quote: Unfortunately, we we actually don't anymore. We've kinda moved away from ground mounts just because of how expensive it it tends to be. You know, it's a lot more to to trench all the equipment into the ground and get the the equipment wired to the home and everything. It just kinda makes it less cost effective. So we wouldn't be able to help out with that.
stewart_read: Parker's response to the ground-mount preference is a single-angle exit delivered with a cost-justification explanation that belongs to the closer tier. The strategic hypothesis — that Parker is deferring to set up a later move — is not supported: there is no probe of why Benjamin prefers ground mount, no curiosity created, and no bridge to any Ion product. The reactive read holds: Parker conceded immediately and explained the business rationale rather than asking a single question. The schema's multi_angle_resilience target is 3+ angles; Parker used one.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have known ground-mount is a hard product gap and chose to be transparent rather than waste Benjamin's time — a reasonable instinct that still fails because it skips the probe entirely.
coaching_implication: Ask Parker: 'Before you told him we don't do ground mounts, what question could you have asked?' The probe that was missing: 'What is it about the roof that you want to avoid?' That answer might have revealed a solvable concern (aesthetics, structural worry, lease situation) or confirmed a true mismatch — but Parker never found out. Run the two-question drill: probe reason before conceding product.

quote: Yeah. Well, it was worth trying. Right? Yeah. Sorry about that. There's a few other companies that I'm sure you can reach out to that might be able to do.
stewart_read: Parker explicitly closes the door and refers Benjamin to competitors after Benjamin's single 'I really don't wanna put it on the roof' line. The valid critique here is single-angle exit and failure to probe the roof-aversion reason before conceding — not anything about the construction timeline, which Benjamin had not yet shared. The strategic hypothesis — that Parker is clearing the call to focus on a better lead — is not supported: Benjamin is a warm, motivated homeowner with a construction project underway. The reactive read holds.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have assessed that without ground-mount capability, there was no product fit and chose to be honest rather than string the customer along.
coaching_implication: The 'it was worth trying' line is the tell — Parker is treating this as a lost cause after one exchange. Show Parker the transcript from 03:43 onward, where Benjamin volunteers a large outbuilding, battery interest, and a future callback. All of that came AFTER Parker had already given up. The coaching question: 'What would you have learned if you'd asked one more question before referring him out?'

quote: Yeah. Because I mean, we do more like residential. So our our kinda main aim is we we match or we we look at your electric bill so to see how much energy you would use, and then we we try to offset your bill with that many panels so we don't — I mean, anything more than that, like, you can get credits for your solar panels, but, the buyback program of, like, power isn't really what it used to be.
stewart_read: Benjamin pushes back on the ground-mount denial with a logical argument about panel capacity. Parker responds with a net-metering explanation that is closer-tier content — bill offset methodology, buyback program mechanics, energy credits vs cash. This is scope creep in response to a customer question rather than a strategic choice. The explanation does not resolve Benjamin's concern (he wanted more panels, not a lesson on net metering) and consumes call time without advancing toward any Ion product.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have been trying to reframe the value proposition to make roof-mount more attractive by explaining how Ion sizes systems to bill offset rather than maximum generation.
coaching_implication: The universal escape hatch from the schema applies here: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer.' Parker's net-metering explanation is not wrong, but it is the wrong tool for this moment. The manager should walk Parker through the scope-creep signals list and ask: 'Which of these did you hit in this call?' The answer is at least two.

quote: Oh, okay. But Okay. I was thinking batteries, you know, run run just so I have power at night or something or if some power goes out, I have power.
stewart_read: Benjamin explicitly raises battery backup as a use case — off-grid power at night and outage protection. This is a direct signal for the Tesla Powerwall, which the schema confirms is the only battery brand Ion carries and a named Ion product. Parker responds at 03:30 by telling Benjamin to 'find someone' — directing a warm customer to competitors on a product Ion sells. This is both a product knowledge failure (Parker may not know Ion carries Powerwall) and a cross-sell miss on a confirmed Ion offering. The strategic hypothesis — that Parker is deferring battery discussion to the closer — is not supported: Parker explicitly says 'find someone else,' not 'our specialist handles that.'
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have understood that without a roof-mount solar install, the Powerwall conversation is premature — Ion may not sell standalone Powerwall without a solar install. If that is Ion's policy, the miss is narrower but the product knowledge gap remains.
coaching_implication: This is the most actionable coaching moment on the call. The manager should confirm with Kenny whether Ion sells standalone Powerwall or only as a solar bundle. If standalone: Parker cost Ion a named-product sale by not knowing the catalog. If bundle-only: Parker still should have said 'we carry the Tesla Powerwall as part of our solar package — let me have our specialist walk you through how that works with a roof-mount system.' Either way, Parker needs a product catalog review before the next call.

quote: Alright. Well, you know, I here's the I'm gonna be working on the house. I I'm I'm kinda about five months to get it completed remodel. Once I'm done, I'm gonna refinance and put up another building on the property that's gonna have a wood shop and three parking spots and, you know, power and a bathroom out there. So when I and that's gonna be bigger than the house.
stewart_read: Benjamin volunteers a detailed construction plan: a remodel completing in five months, a refinance, and a new outbuilding larger than the house with a wood shop, three parking spots, power, and a bathroom. This is a high-load secondary structure on a property with over an acre of land — a direct signal for the TBD 'solar_for_secondary_structure' cross-sell signal in the schema. The outbuilding is new construction, not a replacement scenario, so cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned does not apply directly, but the routing logic is the same: this is an Ion-serviceable opportunity Parker does not surface. Parker's response is a passive 'Cool' and a vague note to call back.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have assessed that the five-month timeline makes any booking premature and chose to note the callback rather than push for an appointment the customer isn't ready for.
coaching_implication: The outbuilding detail is the call's most underworked moment. Parker should have asked: 'That outbuilding — are you planning to run power to it from the grid, or would you want it self-sufficient?' That question keeps Benjamin in the conversation and surfaces whether the outbuilding is a solar opportunity independent of the house. The manager should use this moment to introduce the secondary-structure signal and ask Parker: 'What did you hear Benjamin describe, and what does Ion offer that fits?'

quote: Cool. Well, yeah, I'll make a note and take you out for now. But then, yeah, when we can look out to calling you in a few months or looking out for your call and kinda work with you from there.
stewart_read: Parker closes with a vague 'call you in a few months' note — no locked date, no specific callback time, no confirmation of who calls whom. The schema's soft_outcome_distinction defines the delta between a locked callback with a specific time and a vague 'we'll touch base' as a major predictor of no-show / no-contact rate. This is the softest possible exit: no appointment, no locked callback, no follow-up mechanism. Benjamin's motivation is intact — he closes with 'Alrighty. Cool. Awesome.' — but there is no structural anchor to bring him back.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have correctly assessed that booking an appointment now is premature given the five-month construction timeline and chose to preserve the relationship with a soft note rather than push for a commitment the customer isn't ready to make.
coaching_implication: Even if the appointment is five months out, a locked callback is worth far more than a CRM note. The manager should ask Parker: 'What would it have taken to get a specific date on the calendar — even five months from now?' The suggested move: 'Benjamin, let's do this — I'll put a reminder to reach back out to you in late [month]. What's the best number to reach you on, and is there a day of the week that works better for you?' That converts a vague note into a structured future touch.

quote: I it's also got a little over an acre of land. So I was thinking about using, like, a corner of the property on and put folder on the ground instead of on the roof.
stewart_read: Benjamin's ground-mount preference is not an objection — it is a signal of high engagement and a specific vision. He has thought about the property layout, identified a corner of the acre, and has a rationale (more panels than the roof allows). This is a customer who has done pre-work. Parker treats it as a product mismatch and exits rather than treating it as the emotional anchor the schema's interest_in_solar item is designed to surface. The depth of Benjamin's interest — relocation, construction loan, remodel, acre of land, specific installation vision — is the richest qualify signal on the call and Parker walks past all of it.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have heard 'ground mount only' as a hard constraint that makes the call unworkable, rather than as a preference worth probing.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show Parker what the verify.interest_in_solar item is actually for. Benjamin gave a 60-second answer to 'what got you interested in solar' that contained: relocation, construction project, specific property vision, cost motivation, and a detailed installation preference. A top setter spends 60+ seconds here per the schema. Parker spent zero — he moved straight to the product denial. Ask Parker: 'What did you learn about why Benjamin wants solar?' The answer should be uncomfortable.
